lesson.menu_desc: Put the whole block together in one global meeting simulation: field tough questions and hedge (Q&A), deliver a setback and reassure (damage control), align two factions on a middle ground (consensus), enlist an ally and sway the room (influence), and chair from open to close (leading the room) — consolidating the block's structures: indirect questions, softening language, hypotheticals, and soft imperatives — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 — Block 5 Review — The Global Meeting Simulation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 18 — Block 5 Review

listenings.text: Self-assessment journal, end of block five. Let me be honest with myself about where I stand on these five skills, because I run cross-regional calls every week and I cannot fake my way through them anymore. Handling questions: I have stopped panicking when someone fires a loaded question at me. I buy a moment, I hedge when I am not certain, and I defer the detail and circle back later instead of bluffing. That used to be my weakest point. Damage control: when I have to deliver a setback now, I lead with a soft opener, I state the shortfall in plain active voice, I take accountability, and I reassure people with a real plan to mitigate it. I no longer hide bad news until it explodes. Building consensus: with two factions in the room I try to acknowledge both sides, name the middle ground, and get each side to concede something so we converge. Influence is still hard for me. I can find the win for the other person and enlist an ally before the meeting, but under pressure I slip back into telling people what to do instead of framing it. And chairing: I can open with a purpose, steer the floor, protect the clock, and close with a recap and owners. So my honest read is: strong on questions and damage control, solid on consensus and chairing, and influence is the muscle I still need to build. That is the one I take into next quarter.

Hi Victor, it's Beatriz, with a recap before your global call this afternoon, because this is the meeting where the whole block shows up at once. Five skills, and you will need all of them in one hour. First, questions. When the room pushes you, do not bluff. Hedge what you are unsure of, defer the deep detail, and circle back with numbers after. Second, when something has gone wrong, deliver it cleanly: open softly, name the setback in active voice, own it, and reassure them with how you will mitigate it. People forgive a problem owned far faster than a problem hidden. Third, you will have factions on this call, the regions never fully agree, so do not pick a winner. Acknowledge both sides, find the middle ground, and let each one concede a little so the room converges. Fourth, you cannot order these people, none of them report to you, so influence them: find their win, frame it as theirs, and line up an ally before you even open the call. And fifth, chair it. Own the purpose and the clock, steer the floor, park the digressions, and close with a recap and clear owners. One more thing, and it matters today: people on this call have very different accents, so slow down, confirm what you heard before you respond, and never pretend you understood when you did not. Pull all five together and you will run a genuinely global room. You are ready for this one.
